text: We present our technique for a laparoscopic right hemicolectomy with intracorporeal anastomosis. The patient is a sixteen year old boy who underwent a laparoscopic appendectomy at outside facility. The pathology revealed a one point five centimeter carcinoid tumor with invasion of the peri-appendiceal mesentery. A right hemicolectomy was recommended.

The surgeon evaluates the previous base of the appendix and identifies the ileocolic vessels. The ileocolic vessels are ligated using a ligature device. As dissection continues, the duodenum becomes visible and is mobilized using blunt dissection. The surgeon searches for the right colic artery, which is absent in most patients.

The surgeon carefully dissects within the transverse mesocolon to identify the middle colic vessels, revealing both the right and left branches. The right branch is skeletonized and ligated using a ligature device, while the left branch is preserved. The gallbladder becomes visible, indicating the completion of this section, and the hepatic flexure is then taken down.

A window is created in the mesentery of the terminal ileum, which is then divided using an endo-GIA stapler. The surgeon uses an energy device to continue the dissection of the mesentery cephalad. The right colon is dissected along the white line of Toldt in a standard manner. The transverse colon is prepared for division with an endo-GIA stapler.

The surgeon removes omental attachments from the transverse mesocolon. A side-to-side ileocolonic isoperistaltic anastomosis is created, with enterotomy and decollotomy formed using electrocautery and an energy device. The stapler is positioned to complete the anastomosis, and the defect is closed continuously with absorbable material.
The surgeon closes the mesenteric defect with interrupted absorbable sutures. The specimen is then extracted through a small Pfannenstiel incision.
